title: Learning to Play Again After Complex Trauma
researcherTopic: observation without the observer, freedom from conditioning
metaDescription: For those healing complex trauma, play can feel impossible. Learn why your nervous system resists joy and how to gently rediscover play as a path to healing.
imageDescription: Golden light filters through a window, illuminating dust motes dancing in the air. On a simple wooden table, a child's forgotten wooden toy rests, bathed in a warm, gentle glow. The scene evokes a sense of quiet safety and the possibility of rediscovering simple, forgotten joys.
htmlContent:
Nobody warns you that healing from complex trauma isn't just about processing the pain, but also about rediscovering the parts of you that went dormant—the parts that knew how to play. We often treat play as a luxury, a frivolous activity for children, but for the nervous system shaped by trauma, the absence of play is a profound loss, a silent symptom of a life lived in a state of high alert. The capacity for spontaneous, purposeless joy is a biological imperative, a sign of a nervous system that feels safe enough to come out of hiding. When we’ve spent years, or even decades, with our internal alarms ringing, the silence of safety can feel more deafening than the chaos it replaced, and in that silence, the forgotten language of play waits to be remembered, not as an achievement, but as a homecoming.

The Ghost of Play in a Traumatized System

For a nervous system organized around survival, the concept of play can feel alien, even dangerous. Hypervigilance, the faithful soldier that stood guard for so long, doesn’t simply clock out when the immediate threat is gone; it remains on high alert, scanning the horizon for the next potential danger. This is not a cognitive choice but a deeply embodied state. The world is perceived through a filter of threat, and every interaction, every moment of unstructured time, is assessed for its potential risk. In this state, the very qualities that define play—spontaneity, curiosity, and a lack of clear purpose—are interpreted as vulnerabilities. Why would you let your guard down when the world has proven itself to be unsafe? Why would you engage in an activity without a predictable outcome when unpredictability has so often led to pain?

This is why the well-intentioned advice to "just relax" or "have fun" can land with such a thud, feeling like an impossible demand. It fails to recognize that the body is operating from a different set of instructions, a different rulebook for engagement with the world. A client once described this feeling to me as being in a soundproof room, watching a party through a window. She could see the laughter, the movement, the ease, but she couldn’t hear the music; the felt sense of joy was inaccessible. This is the chasm that complex trauma creates—a separation from the very life force that animates us. We can intellectually understand the concept of play, but the body, in its wisdom, refuses to participate until it has received the necessary signals of safety.

We are not our thoughts, but we are responsible for our relationship to them.

The journey back to play, then, is not about forcing ourselves to have fun. It is about creating the conditions, both internally and externally, where the nervous system can begin to stand down. It is a process of titration, of introducing micro-moments of ease and pleasure, and then waiting, patiently, for the body to recalibrate. It is about learning to listen to the subtle cues of our own system, to honor its resistance, and to gently, persistently, offer it a different experience of the world. It is, in essence, a process of reparenting the parts of ourselves that were never given the chance to simply be.

The Tyranny of "Fun" and the Wisdom of the Freeze

The command to "have fun" is often experienced as a form of tyranny for the trauma survivor. It carries an implicit judgment, a suggestion that our inability to spontaneously erupt in joy is a personal failing, rather than a predictable outcome of a nervous system under siege. This pressure can be profoundly activating, triggering a cascade of shame and self-recrimination that pushes us further away from the very state we are being encouraged to inhabit. We find ourselves performing fun, a hollow imitation of an emotion we cannot genuinely access, which only serves to reinforce the sense of alienation from ourselves and others. This performance is exhausting, and it deepens the divide between our inner world and our outer presentation.

What is often misunderstood is that the inability to play is not a choice; it is a physiological state. For many who have experienced complex trauma, the dominant state is not fight or flight, but freeze—a state of dorsal vagal collapse. This is the body’s most primitive survival strategy, a way of feigning death in the face of overwhelming threat. In this state, the world is muted, distant, and gray. There is a profound sense of disconnection, of being separate from the flow of life. From this place, the vibrant colors of play are simply not visible. The energy required for joyful expression is not available. The system is in a state of conservation, preserving its resources for the most essential functions of survival.

Trauma reorganizes perception. Recovery reorganizes it again, but this time with your participation.

In my years of working in this territory, I have come to see this freeze state not as a pathology to be overcome, but as a source of profound wisdom. It is the body’s way of saying, "I do not feel safe enough for that yet." Our task is not to override this signal, but to honor it. It is to create a container of safety so profound that the frozen parts of ourselves can begin to thaw, slowly, and at their own pace. This is the work of nervous system regulation, of gently rocking the system back into a state of ventral vagal activation, where connection, curiosity, and, eventually, play, become possible again. We must learn to respect the wisdom of the freeze, for it is the guardian of our deepest vulnerabilities.

The Body as the First Playground

The return to play does not begin with a grand gesture or a forced vacation; it begins in the quiet intimacy of our own bodies. It begins with the radical act of paying attention, of turning our awareness inward and simply noticing what is there, without the immediate impulse to fix, change, or judge it. This is the essence of what the philosopher Jiddu Krishnamurti called "observation without the observer"—a way of seeing that is not colored by our past experiences, our beliefs, or our fears. It is a way of meeting the present moment with a quality of open, non-judgmental curiosity. For the trauma survivor, this is a revolutionary act. It is a way of reclaiming the body as a safe and trustworthy home, rather than a source of pain and betrayal.

We can begin this practice in the simplest of ways. Noticing the sensation of our feet on the ground. Feeling the warmth of a cup of tea in our hands. Tracking the subtle rhythm of our own breath as it moves in and out of the body. These are not insignificant acts; they are powerful anchors to the present moment, gentle tethers to the felt sense of being alive. They are the building blocks of what is known as interoception—the ability to feel our own internal state. For so many of us, this capacity has been dulled by years of dissociation, of having to leave our bodies in order to survive. The journey back to ourselves is a journey back into sensation.

Reading about meditation is to meditation what reading the menu is to eating.

As we cultivate this capacity for internal listening, we may begin to notice fleeting moments of ease, of pleasure, of something that feels like a glimmer of play. It might be the way the sunlight falls on a leaf, the sound of a bird outside our window, the simple joy of stretching our limbs after a long period of sitting. These are the "gleams" that therapist Janina Fisher speaks of—the small, often overlooked moments of goodness that are always available to us, even in the midst of our pain. Our task is not to chase these moments, but to notice them, to savor them, to let them land in our nervous system as a new kind of data, a new piece of evidence that the world is not only a place of threat, but also a place of beauty and wonder. This is how we begin to build a new kind of somatic foundation, one that is based not on fear, but on the quiet, steady presence of our own embodied awareness.

Micro-Dosing Joy: The Gentle Return to Spontaneity

The path back to a life that includes play is not a linear ascent but a gentle, often meandering, process of re-acquaintance. It is not about forcing ourselves into situations that feel overwhelming, but about "micro-dosing" joy—introducing small, manageable moments of pleasure and spontaneity that can be integrated by the nervous system without triggering a defensive response. This approach honors the body's pacing, recognizing that trust is rebuilt not through grand declarations but through consistent, reliable experiences of safety and connection. It is a way of gently expanding our window of tolerance, that zone of arousal where we can feel engaged and alive without being overwhelmed or shut down.

What does this look like in practice? It might be as simple as putting on a favorite song and allowing your body to move for a few minutes in whatever way feels good, without any agenda or expectation. It could be taking a different route on your daily walk, just to experience the novelty of a new set of sights and sounds. It might involve spending a few minutes with a pet, fully absorbed in the texture of their fur and the rhythm of their breathing. Or it could be engaging in a creative activity with a focus on the process rather than the product—doodling, coloring, or simply playing with clay. The key is to choose activities that are low-stakes, that have no goal other than the simple pleasure of the experience itself.

The body has a grammar. Most of us never learned to read it.

As we begin to incorporate these small moments of play into our lives, we are, in effect, sending a new message to our nervous system. We are telling it that it is safe to be curious, that it is safe to be spontaneous, that it is safe to experience pleasure. Each time we engage in one of these activities, we are casting a vote for a different way of being in the world. We are strengthening the neural pathways that support connection and ease, and weakening the ones that are wired for threat and defense. This is the essence of neuroplasticity—the brain's remarkable ability to reorganize itself in response to new experiences. It is a slow, incremental process, but it is the foundation upon which a more playful, expansive life can be built. It is about learning to follow the breadcrumbs of our own delight, trusting that they will lead us back to the wholeness that was always our birthright. This is a core tenet of Internal Family Systems (IFS), the recognition that all our parts have a role, and that we can bring them all into a more harmonious relationship.

The Architecture of a Playful Existence

While the return to play begins with small, internal shifts, it is sustained by creating an external environment that supports this new way of being. This is about more than just scheduling "fun" activities; it is about consciously designing a life that has space for unstructured time, for curiosity, and for the kind of gentle, aimless wandering that is the hallmark of a playful spirit. For many of us who have been conditioned to be hyper-productive, to constantly be striving and achieving, this can be a radical and uncomfortable act. It requires us to challenge the deeply ingrained belief that our worth is tied to our output, and to embrace a new paradigm in which being is as valuable as doing.

This might mean setting boundaries around work, carving out time in our schedules that is intentionally left empty, with no agenda other than to see what emerges. It could involve creating a physical space in our homes that is dedicated to creative expression—a corner with art supplies, a comfortable chair for reading, or a space to move and stretch. It might also mean curating our social interactions, choosing to spend time with people who are themselves in touch with their own playful nature, and who can model for us what it looks like to live with a sense of ease and spontaneity. The company we keep has a profound impact on our nervous system; being in the presence of a regulated, playful system can be a powerful co-regulating experience.

Ultimately, building a more playful life is an act of deep self-compassion. It is a recognition that we are not machines, designed for constant productivity, but living organisms that require cycles of rest, renewal, and joyful expression in order to thrive. It is about giving ourselves the permission that we may never have received as children—the permission to be messy, to be imperfect, to make mistakes, and to engage in activities simply for the delight of them. This is not a selfish act, but a necessary one. For it is only when we have replenished our own wells that we have anything of substance to offer the world. A playful life is not a frivolous one; it is a deeply resilient one, capable of meeting the inevitable challenges of existence with a sense of flexibility, creativity, and an unwavering connection to our own vital life force.

From Hypervigilance to Happenstance

The journey from a life defined by hypervigilance to one that has space for happenstance is a profound re-wiring of the self. It is a shift from a world perceived as a series of potential threats to one that is seen as a landscape of possibility. This is not a matter of naive optimism or of ignoring the very real dangers that exist in the world. It is, rather, a recalibration of the nervous system, a return to a state of balance in which we can accurately assess risk without being held captive by it. It is the difference between a system that is constantly screaming "danger!" and one that is able to whisper, "I am safe enough, right now, in this moment."

This shift is the culmination of all the small, consistent efforts we make to befriend our bodies, to listen to our own inner wisdom, and to gently re-introduce moments of play and pleasure into our lives. Each time we choose curiosity over fear, spontaneity over rigidity, and self-compassion over self-criticism, we are laying down a new neural track. We are teaching our brains, on a cellular level, that the world is not only a place of pain, but also a place of connection, of beauty, and of unexpected joy. This is the slow, patient work of building what is often called an "earned secure attachment" with ourselves—a deep, unshakable sense of our own goodness and worth, independent of any external validation.

In my own journey, and in the journeys of those I have had the privilege to walk alongside, I have seen that this shift is not a one-time event, but an ongoing process of returning, again and again, to the present moment. It is a practice of noticing when we have been pulled back into the old story of threat and fear, and gently, without judgment, guiding ourselves back to the felt sense of safety in our own skin. It is a dance between the old patterns and the new possibilities, a gradual tilting of the scales toward a life that feels more spacious, more vibrant, and more authentically our own. The goal is not to eliminate the scars of our past, but to integrate them into a larger, more resilient whole, a self that is capable of holding both the sorrow and the joy, the wound and the wisdom. This is the true meaning of healing—not a return to a state that existed before the trauma, but an arrival into a new, more expansive version of ourselves, one that has room for the full, glorious, and often messy spectrum of human experience.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does trying to have fun make me feel so anxious?

This is a common experience for trauma survivors. Your nervous system has been wired to prioritize safety above all else, and activities that are spontaneous or lack a clear purpose can be interpreted as dangerous. The pressure to "have fun" can feel like a demand to override your body's deepest instincts, leading to anxiety and a sense of alienation. The key is to start with micro-doses of pleasure in a way that feels safe and manageable for your system.

What if I don't know what I find playful or fun anymore?

It is completely normal to lose touch with your sense of play after years of living in survival mode. The first step is not to force it, but to cultivate curiosity. Think back to your childhood—what did you love to do before the world told you who you should be? If that feels inaccessible, simply start by noticing what catches your attention in the present moment. Is it the color of a flower, the sound of a piece of music, the feeling of a soft blanket? Follow those small glimmers of interest without any expectation; they are the breadcrumbs that will lead you back to your own unique sense of play.

How can I play when I still have so much healing to do?

Play is not a reward for being "healed"; it is an essential part of the healing process itself. Play, even in its smallest forms, is a powerful way to regulate your nervous system and build your capacity for resilience. It sends a message to your body that you are safe enough to experience joy, which in turn helps to create a more stable foundation from which to do the deeper work of trauma processing. Think of it not as an "either/or" but a "both/and"—you can hold the grief and the pain of your past while also making space for the lightness and joy of the present.

The journey back to play is, in many ways, the journey back to the essence of who we are beneath the layers of trauma and conditioning. It is a tender and courageous act of self-reclamation. It is the quiet, steady work of remembering that we are not just our wounds; we are also the boundless, creative, and joyful beings that we were always meant to be.

The only question left is whether today is the day.
